accordion_sections.heading: Assessment
accordion_sections.html:
Assess adults with suspected MASLD for fibrosis risk and alternative liver disease causes.

  • History: alcohol intake, metabolic risk factors, medicines.
  • Tests: LFTs, FBC/platelets, HbA1c, lipids, and relevant liver aetiology screen if indicated.
  • Calculate FIB-4 and LiverRisk.
  • Apply referral thresholds: FIB-4 >1.3 if age <65; FIB-4 >2.0 if age ≥65; LiverRisk >10.

accordion_sections.heading: Primary care management
accordion_sections.html:
The default approach is active primary care management.

  • Deliver structured weight-loss support and exercise advice.
  • Optimise diabetes, blood pressure and lipid control.
  • Advise alcohol minimisation/abstinence where liver injury is present.
  • Do not routinely stop or avoid statins solely for mild stable LFT elevation in fatty liver.
  • Use evidence-based obesity/diabetes medicines (including GLP-1 pathway options where indicated) under local formulary rules.
  • Reassess LFTs and fibrosis risk periodically (typically 1–3 yearly by risk profile).

accordion_sections.heading: Who to refer
accordion_sections.html:
Hospital admission (same day)

  • Suspected decompensated liver disease (e.g. jaundice with synthetic dysfunction, ascites, encephalopathy, GI bleeding).

Urgent suspicion of cancer (USOC)

  • Features suspicious of hepatocellular carcinoma/hepatobiliary malignancy.

Urgent (non-cancer outpatient)

  • Marked or rapidly worsening liver derangement with concern for advanced chronic liver disease.

Routine (outpatient)

  • FIB-4 >1.3 (age <65) or FIB-4 >2.0 (age ≥65).
  • LiverRisk >10.
  • Persistent unexplained abnormal LFTs or diagnostic uncertainty.

Include in referral

  • Alcohol history, LFT trend, metabolic comorbidities.
  • FIB-4 and LiverRisk values with dates.
  • Imaging and treatments already started.

accordion_sections.heading: Who not to refer
accordion_sections.html:
Do not routinely refer stable adults with likely MASLD who are below all referral thresholds and have no red-flag features.

  • Continue active primary care management.
  • Repeat risk assessment at clinically appropriate interval.

accordion_sections.html:
